metadata.title: Meet Wilkes The Storytelling Tool
metadata.color_semantics: claude fidelity: INK #3D3929; SPARK #D97757 one accent.
metadata.author: Nik Bear Brown
metadata.credit_line: Nik Bear Brown — Founder, Humanitarians AI
metadata.note: HAI Fellows batch v2 — Teardown register, @NikBearBrown, Remotion animated bookends. Credit: Nik Bear Brown — Founder, Humanitarians AI. HONESTY: all numbers trace to source article.

beats.narration_text: Let's recap with Claude. Wilkes is named for and modeled on journalist Paul Wilkes, whose career shapes how the tool works. Paul Wilkes has authored more than twenty books and appeared in The New Yorker, The Atlantic, and The New York Times Magazine. Because most nonprofits run on stretched time, the workflow is deliberately simple, which is the whole point. A 2006 encounter in India with a deliberately blinded street beggar changed Wilkes and anchors the tool's storytelling purpose.

artifactLines: Wilkes is named for and modeled on journalist Paul Wilkes, whose career shapes how the tool works.
Paul Wilkes has authored more than twenty books and appeared in The New Yorker, The Atlantic, and The New York Times Magazine.
Because most nonprofits run on stretched time, the workflow is deliberately simple, which is the whole point.
A 2006 encounter in India with a deliberately blinded street beggar changed Wilkes and anchors the tool's storytelling purpose.

beats.narration_text: Wilkes being modeled on journalist Paul Wilkes, and the 2006 encounter with a girl deliberately blinded to make her a better beggar, tells me the tool encodes his moral eye for story. I want to see how that becomes software. Can you (1) describe the simple workflow a stretched-thin nonprofit follows to get a story out, (2) map which of Paul Wilkes's journalistic instincts each step is meant to reproduce, and (3) draft the exact prompts Wilkes would use to turn a raw field note into a New Yorker-style narrative?

output: He still lives in Wilmington, North Carolina, and still writes

beats.narration_text: Wilkes is named for, and modeled on, the journalist Paul Wilkes, and his story is the reason the tool works the way it does. Paul Wilkes is a journalist, author of more than twenty books, and documentarian whose work has appeared in The New Yorker, The Atlantic, and The New York Times Magazine.

beats.narration_text: He still lives in Wilmington, North Carolina, and still writes. In 2006, on a trip to India, he encountered a street girl who had been deliberately blinded to make her a “better beggar.” That moment changed everything.

beats.narration_text: Your turn. Paste this into Claude — Take the central idea from 'Meet Wilkes The Storytelling Tool' and give me the three most actionable takeaways — one sentence… That's the prompt that actually tests whether you got it. Run it and see where it surprises you.

props.command: Take the central idea from 'Meet Wilkes The Storytelling Tool' and give me the three most actionable takeaways — one sentence each. Then name the failure mode I'm most likely to hit.
